title: Intrinsic Floquet Generation and $1/I$ Quantum Oscillations in a Sliding Charge-Density Wave
abstract: The realization of intrinsic, tunable high-frequency quantum states without external radiation is a major goal in condensed matter physics and quantum device engineering. Here, we demonstrate that a uniformly sliding charge-density wave (CDW) acts as an intrinsic dc-to-ac converter, transforming spatial periodicity into temporal periodicity to realize a unique periodically driven quantum state. We show that the isolated sliding-CDW problem is exactly solvable in Floquet form, yielding split gap edges and a ladder of Floquet sidebands. Using this exact solution, we reveal that weak-probe tunneling spectroscopy naturally yields an inverse-current ($1/I$) oscillation as a fixed-bias cut of the sideband ladder. Matching the observed oscillation period to theory indicates that the macroscopic current must percolate through a highly localized coherent filament, with an effective channel number orders of magnitude smaller than the geometric chain count. Furthermore, using a segmented multiterminal model, we demonstrate that inelastic phase-slip dephasing near the contacts explains the strong suppression of oscillation visibility on outer voltage probes. Ultimately, our results provide a rigorous transport interpretation of the striking $1/I$ quantum oscillations recently observed in quasi-one-dimensional CDW insulators. More broadly, they highlight a universal spatial-to-temporal conversion mechanism where the insulating gap protects Floquet coherence, offering a novel paradigm for intrinsically driven quantum devices.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework to explain 1/I quantum oscillations in sliding charge-density waves as a result of intrinsic Floquet sideband generation. It reveals that the observed oscillations imply conduction through highly localized coherent filaments and offers a new paradigm for creating intrinsically driven quantum devices.
main_problem: The paper aims to explain the origin of 1/I (inverse-current) quantum oscillations observed in quasi-one-dimensional charge-density wave (CDW) insulators and to understand how a sliding CDW acts as an intrinsic dc-to-ac converter.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a sliding CDW generates a Floquet sideband ladder, where 1/I oscillations arise from the periodic crossing of these sidebands with the probe chemical potential. They also show that the current must flow through a highly localized coherent filament (N_eff << N_geom) and that dephasing near contacts suppresses oscillation visibility in multi-terminal setups.
method: The study uses Floquet theory and the Schwinger-Keldysh Green's function formalism to solve the time-dependent Schrödinger equation for a sliding CDW. They employ a segmented multi-terminal model and a weak-probe tunneling spectroscopy approach to simulate transport observables.
model_or_system: The system is a quasi-1D charge-density wave (CDW) insulator in a sliding state. The model uses a single-band, two-branch mean-field Hamiltonian and treats the sliding phase as a time-dependent density modulation that creates a Floquet spectrum.
key_observables: Differential conductance (dI/dV), probe current, terminal-pair voltages (V_p-q), and the Floquet spectral function.
important_parameters: Sliding frequency (Omega), CDW gap (Delta), effective channel number (N_eff), contact mismatch (epsilon), and temperature (T).
main_assumptions: The CDW is treated as a fully gapped insulator to minimize inelastic dephasing; the system is modeled in the weak-probe limit for spectroscopy; and the current is assumed to be driven through a localized coherent filament.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows a schematic of a sliding-CDW device; Fig 2 illustrates a segmented multi-terminal model; Fig 3 displays the Floquet spectral function with split gap edges; Fig 8 shows the oscillatory kernel in inner vs. outer terminal voltages.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the problem of 1/I oscillations, then develops the Floquet Hamiltonian and exact solution for a sliding CDW. It proceeds to model tunneling spectroscopy and multi-terminal transport, compares theoretical predictions to experimental parameters (like N_eff), and concludes with a discussion on the spatial-to-temporal conversion mechanism.
why_interesting: This paper is interesting because it demonstrates a universal mechanism for spatial-to-temporal conversion and the generation of intrinsic high-frequency Floquet states without external radiation, which is highly relevant to the study of driven many-body dynamics and the engineering of intrinsic quantum drives.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Finite sliding-CDW device coupled to left and right metallic leads. This two-terminal geometry is distinct from the weak single- probe spectroscopy setup discussed in the text.

FIG. 2. Experiment-motivated segmented model for a persistent- current-driven multiterminal device. One imposed current Iset flows through three serial segments. The central segment 2-3 hosts the coherent Floquet kernel Oosc(I, T), whereas the outer segments con- tribute smooth, effectively dephased voltage drops. The experimen- tally relevant inner- and outer-terminal voltages are indicated above the device.

FIG. 4. Weak single-contact tunneling geometry. A metallic probe P is weakly tunnel-coupled to a local region of the sliding CDW and biased by VP relative to the local CDW potential. The probe current ⟨JP⟩samples the local Floquet spectrum, while the sliding steady state and frequency Ω(I) are maintained elsewhere by the external dc drive and relaxation of the CDW.

FIG. 5. Schematic of the percolating sliding filament. To match the experimentally observed oscillation period, the macroscopic terminal-to-terminal current I must funnel through a highly localized active region. The effective coherent channel number Neff is orders of magnitude smaller than the geometric chain count Ngeom of the pinned bulk.

FIG. 6. Fixed-bias inverse-current oscillations generated by the same Floquet sideband ladder. Panel (a) shows the individually normalized oscillatory contribution δGosc(I, ϵ; T) at the reference offset ϵ = 10 meV as a function of 1/I.
